senses.g: a collection (of items, group, people etc.) — sum, total
a collection (of items, group, people etc.) — group
a collection (of items, group, people etc.) — a crowd; assembly
set (ie. an aggregate; set theory, collection of objects)

مجموعہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَجْمُوعَه (majmū'a), borrowed from Arabic مَجْمُوعَة (majmūʕa), from مَجْمُوع (majmūʕ), from جَمَعَ (jamaʕa).

senses.g: Qays ibn al-Mullawah (the hero of the romance Layla and Majnun)

مجنون.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَجْنُون (majnūn), from Arabic مَجْنُون (majnūn, “crazy”, literally “possessed by a jinn”).
Borrowed from Classical Persian مَجْنُون (majnūn), from Arabic مَجْنُون (majnūn, “crazy”, literally “possessed by a jinn”).

senses.g: Romeo (a man who is a great lover)

مجنون.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَجْنُون (majnūn), from Arabic مَجْنُون (majnūn, “crazy”, literally “possessed by a jinn”).

senses.g: passive voice, where the agent is unknown.
a foreign, unknown vowel — an epithet for the diacritics
a foreign, unknown vowel — an epithet for the و (vao) and ی (ye) letters when representing the sounds /oː/ and /eː/, which are absent in Arabic.

مجہول.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مجهول (majhūl), from Arabic مَجْهُول (majhūl).

مجھ.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Apabhramsa मज्झु (majjhu), from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀫𑀚𑁆𑀛𑀁 (majjhaṃ), from Sanskrit मह्यम् (mahyam). Cognate with Marathi माझे (māj̈he).

senses.g: Machh (a city in Kachhi district, Balochistan, Pakistan)

مچھ.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀫𑀘𑁆𑀙 (maccha), from Sanskrit मत्स्य (mátsya).
Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀫𑀘𑁆𑀙 (maccha), from Sanskrit मत्स्य (mátsya).

مچھر.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀫𑀘𑁆𑀙𑀭 (macchara), from Sanskrit मत्सर (matsara). Compare Gujarati મચ્છર (macchar).

مچھلی.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀫𑀘𑁆𑀙 (maccha), from Sanskrit मत्स्य (matsya). This -𑀮- (-la-) extended form originating in Middle Indo-Aryan appears to have spread from Eastern Hindi.

senses.g: defending, protecting, guarding; keeping in custody

محافظت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian محافظت (muhāfazat), from Arabic مُحَافَظَة (muḥāfaẓa).

محال.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُحَال (muhāl), borrowed from Arabic مُحَال (muḥāl), from أَحَالَ (ʔaḥāla). First attested in c. 1657 as Middle Hindi محال (mḥal /⁠muhāl⁠/).

محبت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُحَبَّت (muhabbat), borrowed from Arabic مَحَبَّة (maḥabba), from حَبَّ (ḥabba). First attested in c. 1564 as Middle Hindi محبت (mḥbt).

محتاط.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُحْتَاط (muhtāt), borrowed from Arabic مُحْتَاط (muḥtāṭ).

محترم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُحْتَرَم (muhtaram), in turn borrowed from Arabic مُحْتَرَم (muḥtaram), passive participle of اِحْتَرَمَ (iḥtarama), from the root ح ر م (ḥ r m).

محتسب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian محتسب (muhtasib), from Arabic مُحْتَسِب (muḥtasib), active participle of اِحْتَسَبَ (iḥtasaba).

محراب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian محراب (mihrāb), from Arabic مِحْرَاب (miḥrāb).

محروم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian محروم (mahrūm), from Arabic مَحْرُوم (maḥrūm).

محسن.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُحْسِن (muhsin), from Arabic مُحْسِن (muḥsin).
Borrowed from Classical Persian مُحْسِن (muhsin), from Arabic مُحْسِن (muḥsin).

senses.g: a male given name, Mohsin, from Arabic

محسن.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُحْسِن (muhsin), from Arabic مُحْسِن (muḥsin).

محسوس.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian محسوس (maḥsūs), itself from Arabic مَحْسُوس (maḥsūs).

محشر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian محشر (mahšar), from Arabic مَحْشَر (maḥšar, “place of assembly; last judgment”).

محفل.e: Borrowed from Arabic مَحْفِل (maḥfil, “assembly, congregation”), from the root ح ف ل (ḥ f l).

محل.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian محل (mahall), from Arabic مَحَلّ (maḥall).

محلہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَحَلَّه (mahalla), borrowed from Arabic مَحَلَّة (maḥalla), from حَلَّ (ḥalla). Compare Bengali মহল্লা (moholla) and Punjabi مَحَلَّہ (maḥallah) / ਮਹੱਲਾ (mahallā).

senses.g: a male given name, Muhammad, Mohammed, or Mohammad, from Arabic

محمد.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُحَمَّد (muhammad), from Arabic مُحَمَّد (muḥammad).

senses.g: a male given name from Chagatai

محمد قلی.e: From Chagatai محمدقلی (mḥmdqly, literally “slave of Muhammad”); compare Azerbaijani Məhəmmədqulu, Turkish Muhammedkulu.

senses.g: pertaining to Muhammad, for example in comparison with other prophets.

محنت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مِحْنَت (mihnat), borrowed from Arabic مِحْنَة (miḥna). First attested in c. 1635 as Middle Hindi محنت (mḥnt /⁠mihnat⁠/).

senses.g: a male given name from Arabic

محیی الدین.e: Ultimately derived from Arabic مُحْيِي الدِّين (muḥyī d-dīn, literally “Renewer of the Faith”).

مخالف.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُخَالِف (muxālif), borrowed from Arabic مُخَالِف (muḵālif), from خَالَفَ (ḵālafa). First attested in c. 1528 as Middle Hindi مخالف (mxalf /⁠muxālif⁠/).
Borrowed from Classical Persian مُخَالِف (muxālif), borrowed from Arabic مُخَالِف (muḵālif), from خَالَفَ (ḵālafa). First attested in c. 1528 as Middle Hindi مخالف (mxalf /⁠muxālif⁠/).

مخالفت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُخَالِفَت (muxālifat), borrowed from Arabic مُخَالَفَة (muḵālafa), from خَالَفَ (ḵālafa).

مختصر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مختصر (muxtasar), from Arabic مُخْتَصَر (muḵtaṣar).

مختصرا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مختصراً (muxtasaran), from Arabic مُخْتَصَرًا (muḵtaṣaran), adverbial accusative of مُخْتَصَر (muḵtaṣar).

مختلف.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مختلف and Arabic مُخْتَلِف (muḵtalif). Compare Punjabi مُخْتَلَف (muxtalaf) / ਮੁਖ਼ਤਲਫ਼ (muxtalaf).
Borrowed from Arabic مُخْتَلَف (muḵtalaf) from the root خ ل ف (ḵ l f).

مخزن.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مخزن (maxzan), from Arabic مَخْزَن (maḵzan).

مخلوط.e: Borrowed from Arabic مَخْلُوط (maḵlūṭ), passive participle of خَلَطَ (ḵalaṭa, “to mix”).

مخمل.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مخمل (maxmal), from Arabic مُخْمَل (muḵmal).

مد نظر.e: Borrowed from Arabic مَدِّ نَظَر (maddi naẓar).
Borrowed from Arabic مَدِّ نَظَر (maddi naẓar).

مدد.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَدَد (madad), borrowed from Arabic مَدَد (madad). First attested in c. 1611 as Middle Hindi مدد (mdd /⁠madad⁠/).

senses.g: Chennai (a megacity, the capital of Tamil Nadu, India)
Name of Madras Province (Present day Andhara Pradesh, Tamil Nadu and Parts of Kerala and Karnataka

مدرسہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مدرسه (madrasa), from Arabic مَدْرَسَة (madrasa).

مدعو.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَدْعُو (mad'ū), borrowed from Arabic مَدْعُوّ (madʕuww), from دَعَا (daʕā).

senses.g: a claimant, suitor; plaintiff (in a law-suit), complainant, prosecutor, accuser
a pretender, pretentious man; a boaster

مدعی.e: Borrowed from Arabic مُدَّعِي (muddaʕī), the informal pronunciation of مُدَّعٍ (muddaʕin), active participle of اِدَّعَى (iddaʕā).

senses.g: asserted as a claim, claimed, sued for; alleged; pretended; meant

مدعی.e: Borrowed from Arabic مُدَّعَى (muddaʕā), passive participle of اِدَّعَى (iddaʕā).

senses.g: what is claimed, or alleged, or pretended, or meant; desire, wish; suit; meaning, object, view; scope, tenor, drift

مدعی.e: Borrowed from Arabic مُدَّعَى (muddaʕā), passive participle of اِدَّعَى (iddaʕā).

مدفون.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَدْفُون (madfūn), from Arabic مَدْفُون (madfūn).

مدہوش.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَدْهوش (madhōš). By surface analysis, مَد (mad) + ہوش (hoś).

senses.g: wine, liquor - a strong or intoxicating drink
Medina (a major city, the capital of Medina province, Saudi Arabia; holy to Islam)

مدینہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مدینه (madīna), itself from Arabic الْمَدِينَة (al-madīna).

مدھم.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀫𑀤𑁆𑀥𑀫 (maddhama), from Sanskrit मध्यम (madhyama).

senses.g: name of the fourth musical note out of seven notes.

مدھم.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀫𑀤𑁆𑀥𑀫 (maddhama), from Sanskrit मध्यम (madhyama).

senses.g: a female given name, Madhu, from Sanskrit
a diminutive, Madhu, of the female given name مادَھوی (mādhavī)

مدھو.e: Borrowed from Sanskrit मधु (madhu). Doublet of مَے (mai, “liquor, wine”).
Borrowed from Sanskrit मधु (madhu). Doublet of مَے (mai, “liquor, wine”).

senses.g: Madhya Pradesh (a state in central India)
Madagascar (an island and country off the east coast of Africa)

ex.u: میں اسکا برا مذاق برداشت نہیں کروںگا۔
ex.e: I will not tolerate his bad joke.

مذاق.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَذَاق (mazāq), from Arabic مَذَاق (maḏāq).

مذکر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُذَکَّر (muzakkar), from Arabic مُذَكَّر (muḏakkar).
Borrowed from Classical Persian مُذَکَّر (muzakkar), from Arabic مُذَكَّر (muḏakkar).

مذکور.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَذْکُور (mazkūr), from Arabic مَذْكُور (maḏkūr). First attested in c. 1635 Middle Hindi مذكور (mazkūr).
Borrowed from Classical Persian مَذْکُور (mazkūr), from Arabic مَذْكُور (maḏkūr). First attested in c. 1635 Middle Hindi مذكور (mazkūr).

مذمت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مذمت (mazammat), from Arabic مَذَمَّة (maḏamma).

ex.u:
مذہب نہیں سکھاتا آپس میں بیر رکھنا
ہندی ہیں ہم وطن ہے ہندوستاں ہمارا
mazhab nahī̃ sikhātā, āpas mẽ bair rakhnā
hindī ha͠i ham, vatan ha͠i, hindostā̃ hamārā
Religion does not preach animosity among ourselves.
ex.r: We are Hindi and our homeland is Hindustan.

senses.g: madhhab, school of thought in Islamic jurisprudence

مذہب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَذْهَب (mazhab), from Arabic مَذْهَب (maḏhab).

مذہبی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مذهبی (mazhabī). Equivalent to مذہب (mazhab, “religion, faith”) + ـی (-ī).

ex.u: مجھے معلوم نہیں تھا کی تم مراٹھی بول سکتے ہو۔
ex.r: mujhe mālūm nahī̃ thā ki tum marāṭhī bol sakte ho.
ex.e: I didn't know that you can speak Marathi.

مراد.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُرَاد (murād), from Arabic مُرَاد (murād).

senses.g: Marrakech (the capital city of the Marrakesh-Safi region, Morocco)

مراکش.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَرَاکِش (marākiš), from Arabic مَرَّاكِش (marrākiš).

مربا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مربا (murabbā), from Arabic مُرَبَّى (murabbā). Cognate with Bengali মোরব্বা (mōrobba).

مرت.e: Borrowed from Sanskrit मृत (mṛta). Doublet of مردہ (murdā).

مرتبہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مرتبه (martaba), from Arabic مَرْتَبَة (martaba, “status, degree, place, division”).

ex.u: جیون اور مرتیو کا سوال ہے۔
ex.r: jīvan aur mrityu kā savāl hai.
ex.e: It is a question of life and death.

senses.g: personification of Yama, the God of death
a marsiya (an elegiac poem written to commemorate the martyrdom and valour of Hussain ibn Ali and his comrades of the Karbala' in the Battle of Karbala)
a lamentation for one dead, an elegy, a dirge, an epideceum (especially the one sung during the Muharram in commemoration of Hasan and Husayn)

مرثیہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَرْثِیَه (marsiya), from Arabic مَرْثِيَة (marṯiya).

مرچ.e: Inherited from Sanskrit मरीच (marīca), from मरिच (marica).

senses.g: Mardan (a city in Mardan district, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, Pakistan)

مردد.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُرَدَّد (muraddad), from Arabic مُرَدَّد (muraddad).
Borrowed from Classical Persian مُرَدِّد (muraddid), from Arabic مُرَدِّد (muraddid).
Borrowed from Classical Persian مُرَدِّد (muraddid), from Arabic مُرَدِّد (muraddid).

مردہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُرْدَه (murda). Doublet of مَرِت (marit).
Borrowed from Classical Persian مُرْدَه (murda). Doublet of مَرِت (marit).

مردہ باد.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مرده باد, from مرده (murda, “dead”) + باد (bād, “may ... be”).

senses.g: a dry or desert tract of country

مرض.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَرَض (maraz), from Arabic مَرَض (maraḍ).

مرضی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَرْضِی (marzī), from Arabic مُرْضِي (murḍī, “pleasing”).
Borrowed from Classical Persian مَرْضِی (marzī), from Arabic مُرْضِي (murḍī, “pleasing”).

مرغابی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مرغابی (murġābī). By surface analysis, مُرْغ (murġ) + آبِی (ābī).

senses.g: stroke (written above the letter kāf or twice on the letter gāf)

مرکز.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مرکز (markaz), itself from Arabic مَرْكَز (markaz).

مرکزی.e: Borrowed from Arabic مَرْكَزِيّ (markaziyy). By surface analysis, مرکز (markaz, “center”) + ـی (-ī).

ex.u:
سکیاں میں تو ہے مرگ نینی چھبیلی
سجن تو نہیں ہوتے تج تھے کنارے
sikyā̃ mẽ tū ha͠i mirg nainī chabelī
ex.r: sajan tū nahī̃ hote tuj the kināre
ex.e: (please add an English translation of this quotation)

senses.g: A place where Hindus burn their dead

مرگھٹ.e: Compounded from مرا (marā, “dead”) + گھاٹ (ghāṭ, “area close to a body of water”)

مرمت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مرمت (marammat), Arabic مَرَمَّة (maramma). Compare Punjabi مُرَمَّت (murammat) and Nepali मर्मत (marmat).

مرنا.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀫𑀭𑀇 (maraï), from Sanskrit मर॑ति (márati).

senses.g: to have or get killed or dead

ex.e: You would've got me killed today for sure!

مروانا.e: Causative form of مرنا (marnā) with stem augmented by وا (vā)

مریخ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مِرِّیخ (mirrīx), borrowed from Arabic الْمِرِّيخ (al-mirrīḵ). First attested in c. 1611 as Middle Hindi مریخ (mryx /⁠mirrīx⁠/).

senses.g: Murdike (a city in Sheikhupura district, Punjab, Pakistan)

مریض.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مریض (marīz), from Arabic مَرِيض (marīḍ).

senses.g: Miriam (the sister of Moses and Aaron and the daughter of Amram and Jochebed)
a female given name, Maryam or Mariyam

مریم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَرْیَم (maryam), from Arabic مَرْيَم (maryam), from Aramaic מרים (maryam).
Borrowed from Classical Persian مَرْیَم (maryam), from Arabic مَرْيَم (maryam), from Aramaic מרים (maryam).

مزاج.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مزاج (mizāj), from Arabic مِزَاج (mizāj, “temperament”).

مزاحمت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مزاحمت (muzāhamat), from Arabic مُزاحَمَتْ (muzāḥamat).

مزدور.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مزدور (muzdūr, “mercenary; labourer”), which in turn inherited from Middle Persian [script needed] (myzdwbl /⁠mizdwar⁠/, “hireling, hired laborer”).

مزید.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَزِید (mazīd), from Arabic مَزِيد (mazīd). Compare Punjabi مَزِید (mazīd) / ਮਜ਼ੀਦ (mazīd).
Borrowed from Classical Persian مَزِید (mazīd), from Arabic مَزِيد (mazīd). Compare Punjabi مَزِید (mazīd) / ਮਜ਼ੀਦ (mazīd).

مژگانی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مژگان (muž(a)gān, miž(a)gān), plural of مژه (muža, miža, “eyelash”).

مسافر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُسَافِر (musāfir), borrowed from Arabic مُسَافِر (musāfir). First attested in c. 1657 as Middle Hindi مسافر (msafr /⁠musāfir⁠/).

مستحق.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُسْتَحَقّ (mustahaqq), from Arabic مُسْتَحَقّ (mustaḥaqq).

مستحکم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُسْتَحْکَم (mustahkam), from Arabic مُسْتَحْكَم (mustaḥkam).

senses.g: a place of rest and repose, lounge

مسترد.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مسترد (mustaradd), from Arabic مُسْتَرَدّ (mustaradd).

senses.g: one seeking to be excused, relieved, or exempted from

مستقبل.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُسْتَقْبِل (mustaqbil), from Arabic مُسْتَقْبَل (mustaqbal).
Borrowed from Classical Persian مُسْتَقْبِل (mustaqbil), from Arabic مُسْتَقْبَل (mustaqbal).

senses.g: a place for planes, trains, and buses to dwell, station, stop

مستقل.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُسْتَقِل (mustaqil), from Arabic مُسْتَقِلّ (mustaqill).

senses.g: a district in northwestern Balochistan, Pakistan

مسٹنڈا.e: Unknown. Possibly مَسْتَان (mastān, “drunkard, rogue, thug”) contaminated by غُنْڈَہ (ġunḍa, “thug”).
Unknown. Possibly مَسْتَان (mastān, “drunkard, rogue, thug”) contaminated by غُنْڈَہ (ġunḍa, “thug”).

senses.g: a place of worship for prostration

ex.u: اس عبادت گاہ کو بنی اسرائیل مسجد بھی کہا جاتا تھا۔
ex.r: karācī meṉ muqīm yahūdiyoṉ kī tārīx
ex.e: This place of worship also used to be known as the Bene Israel synagogue.

مسجد.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَسْجِد (masjid), borrowed from Arabic مَسْجِد (masjid), from سَجَدَ (sajada). First attested in c. 1609 as Middle Hindi مسجد (msjd /⁠masjid⁠/). Displaced Urdu مَسِیت (masīt).

مسلط.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُسَلَّط (musallat), borrowed from Arabic مُسَلَّط (musallaṭ), from سَلَّطَ (sallaṭa).

مسلم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُسْلِم (muslim), borrowed from Arabic مُسْلِم (muslim), from أَسْلَمَ (ʔaslama).
Borrowed from Classical Persian مُسْلِم (muslim), borrowed from Arabic مُسْلِم (muslim), from أَسْلَمَ (ʔaslama).

senses.g: Muslim Bagh (a city in Qilla Saifullah district, Balochistan, Pakistan)

مسلم باغ.e: From مسلم + باغ (bāġ), previously known as Hindu Bagh.

مسلمان.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُسَلْمَان (musalmān), from Arabic مُسْلِم (muslim). First attested as Middle Hindi مسلمان (musalamān), Old Hindi मुसलमांन (musalamāṃna).

senses.g: alternative form of مَسُوڑھا (masūṛhā, “gum (of the teeth)”)

مسوڑھا.e: Possibly from Sanskrit *मांसपुटक (*māṃsapuṭaka), from मांस (māṃsa) + पुट (puṭa) + -क (-ka).

senses.g: vocative plural of مَسُوڑھا (masūṛhā, “gum (of the teeth)”)
oblique plural of مَسُوڑھا (masūṛhā, “gum (of the teeth)”)
inflection of مَسُوڑھا (masūṛhā, “gum (of the teeth)”): — direct plural
inflection of مَسُوڑھا (masūṛhā, “gum (of the teeth)”): — oblique/vocative singular

مسیح.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَسِیح (masīh), from Arabic مَسِيح (masīḥ, “anointed, messiah”).

مسیح موعود.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مسیح موعود (masīḥ-e mau'ūd). By surface analysis, مسیح (masīh, “messiah”) + موعود (mau'ūd, “promised; predicted”).

senses.g: Christian; relating to Christianity or Christ.

مسیحیت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَسِیحِیَّت (masīhiyyat), from Arabic مَسِيحِيَّة (masīḥiyya). By surface analysis, مَسِیحی (masīhī, “Christian”) + ـِیَت (-iyat, “-ity”).

مشاور.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُشَاوِر (mušāwir), from Arabic مُشَاوِر (mušāwir).

مشاورت.e: From Classical Persian مشاورت (mušāwarat), from Arabic مُشَاوَرَة (mušāwara).

مشاہدہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُشَاهِدَه (mušāhida), borrowed from Arabic مُشَاهَدَة (mušāhada), from شَاهَدَ (šāhada).

مشت خاک.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مشت خاک (must-i xāk), from مشت (muśt, “fist”) + خاک (xāk, “dust”).

ex.u: کچھ دیر تک باہر دیکھتا رہا۔ سامنے والے مکانوں کی مشترکہ چھت پر چند کبوتر بیٹھے تھے
ex.e: He kept watching for some time. There were some pigeons perched on the shared roof of the opposite houses.

مشترکہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُشْتَرَکَه (muštaraka), itself from Arabic مُشْتَرَكَة (muštaraka, “shared”).

مشترکہ منڈی.e: Calque of English Common Market, from مُشْتَرَکَہ (muśtaraka, “shared; joint”) + مَنْڈی (manḍī, “market; souq”).

مشتری.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مشتری (muštarī), from Arabic مُشْتَرٍ (muštarin).
Borrowed from Classical Persian مشتری (muštarī), from Arabic الْمُشْتَرِي (al-muštarī).

senses.g: a letter with a tashdid diacritic.

مشدد.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُشَدَّد (mušaddad), from Arabic مُشَدَّد (mušaddad).

مشرق.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مشرق, from Arabic مَشْرِق (mašriq).

senses.g: Middle East (the geopolitical region at the intersection of Asia, Africa and Europe, comprising West Asia excluding the South Caucasus and additionally including all of Egypt and all of Turkey)

مشرق وسطی.e: Calque of English Middle East, with مشرق (maśriq, “east”) + ِ (-i, “izafat construction”) + وسطی (vust̤ā, “middle”).

senses.g: East Asia (a geographic region comprising the eastern part of Asia, inclusive of China proper, Japan, North Korea, South Korea, and Taiwan and sometimes inclusive of Mongolia and Tibet and some or all of Southeast Asia and the Philippines)
East Pakistan (a former province of Pakistan, now the independent country of Bangladesh)
East Timor (a country and island of Southeast Asia; capital: Dili)

مشرک.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُشْرِک (mušrik), from Arabic مُشْرِك (mušrik).

senses.g: a sort of cresset; a torch, flambeau

مشغلہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مشغله (mašġala), from Arabic مَشْغَلة (mašḡala), from the root ش غ ل (š ḡ l).

مشغول.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَشْغُول (mašġūl), from Arabic مَشْغُول (mašḡūl). Compare Punjabi مَشْغُول (maśġūl) / ਮਸ਼ਗ਼ੂਲ (maśġūl), Sindhi مَشْغُول (maśġūl) / मशग़ूल (maśġūl), Gujarati મશગૂલ (maśgūl), Bengali মশগুল (mośgul).

مشق.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَشْق (mašq), Arabic مَشْق (mašq).

مشقت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مشقت (mašaqqat), itself from Arabic مَشَقَّة (mašaqqa).

مشکل.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مشکل (muškil), from Arabic مُشْكِل (muškil).
Borrowed from Classical Persian مشکل (muškil), from Arabic مُشْكِل (muškil).

مشکوک.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَشْکُوک (maškūk), Arabic مَشْكُوك (maškūk).

مشورہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَشْوَرَه (mašwara), borrowed from Arabic مَشْوَرَة (mašwara).

senses.g: direct plural/ oblique singular/ vocative singular of مَشْوَرَہ (maśvara, “advice”)
a place where a martyr has died, or is buried; a place of martyrdom; a place of religious visitation; a tomb
a place of assembling, an assembly
Mashhad (a city in Iran, the seat of Mashhad County's Central District and the capital of Razavi Khorasan Province)

مشہور.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مشهور (mašhūr), from Arabic مَشْهُور (mašhūr, “famous, notorious”).

مصالحہ.e: From Classical Persian مصالح (maṣāliḥ, “affairs, materials, spices”), a plural of مصلحت (maslahat), itself borrowing from Arabic مَصْلَحَة (maṣlaḥa).

senses.g: Egypt (a country in North Africa and West Asia)

ex.u: مصر جانے کو جی مچلتا ہے
ex.e: My heart yearns to go to Egypt

senses.g: cities south of Cairo in Egypt.

مصر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مِصْر (misr), from Arabic مِصْر (miṣr).
Borrowed from Classical Persian مِصْر (misr), from Arabic مِصْر (miṣr).

مصری.e: Borrowed from Arabic مِصْرِيّ (miṣriyy). By surface analysis, مصر (misr) + ـی (-ī).
Borrowed from Arabic مِصْرِيّ (miṣriyy). By surface analysis, مصر (misr) + ـی (-ī).

senses.g: calamity, disaster, catastrophe (a sudden event that causes great damage)

مصیبت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُصِیبَت (musībat), from Arabic مُصِيبَة (muṣība).

مضر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مضر, from Arabic مُضِرّ (muḍirr).

مضموم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مضموم (mazmūm), from Arabic مَضْمُوم (maḍmūm).

مضمون.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مضمون (mazmūn), from Arabic مَضْمُون (maḍmūn).

مطالعہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مطالعه (mutāla'a), from Arabic مُطَالَعَة (muṭālaʕa).
